The Makepeace
Marketing Multiplier
Like finding gold in your back yard …
Module 4

-- 1 of 38 --

The Makepeace Marketing Multiplier
Three steps to creating explosive growth
STEP 	1 S 	t 	ti 	ll 	lti l 	th 	NUMBER 	f ti
Three steps to creating explosive growth
in sales, profits and especially in your income.
STEP #1: Systematically multiply the NUMBER of times
each customer buys from you ...
STEP #2: Systematically multiply the AMOUNT each	STEP #2: Systematically multiply the AMOUNT each
customer spends with you each time and ...
STEP #3: Systematically increase the TIME during	STEP #3: Systematically increase the TIME during
which each customer buys from you.
(Sounds easy when you say it fast – right?)

-- 2 of 38 --

Increasing the NUMBER of Purchases Each
The Makepeace Marketing Multiplier
Increasing the NUMBER of Purchases Each
Customer Makes Per Year …
… Multiplies Annual Sales and Customer
Lifetime Value:	Lifetime Value:
Purchases 	Average 	Annual 	Lifetime 	Lifetime
Per Year 	Sale 	Revenue 	In Years 	Value
1 	$100 	$100 	4 	$400
2 	$100 	$200 	4 	$800
3 	$100 	$300 	4 	$1,200
4 	$100 	$400 	4 	$1,600
$100 	4 	$2 000	5 	$100 	$500 	4 	$2,000
6 	$100 	$600 	4 	$2,400

-- 3 of 38 --

Increasing the SIZE of the Purchases Each
The Makepeace Marketing Multiplier
Increasing the SIZE of the Purchases Each
Customer Makes …
… Multiplies Annual Sales and Customer
Lifetime Value
AGAIN:
Purchases 	Average 	Annual 	Lifetime 	Lifetime
Per Year 	Sale 	Revenue 	(In Years) 	Value
3 	$100 	$300 	4 	$1,200
3 	$150 	$450 	4 	$1,800
3 	$200 	$600 	4 	$2,400
3 	$250 	$750 	4 	$3,000
3 	$300 	$900 	4 	$3 600	3 	$300 	$900 	4 	$3,600
3 	$350 	$1,050 	4 	$4,200

-- 4 of 38 --

Increasing the TIME During Which
The Makepeace Marketing Multiplier
Increasing the TIME During Which
Each Customer Buys From You …
… Multiplies Customer Lifetime Value
YET AGAIN:
Purchases 	Average 	Annual 	Lifetime 	Lifetime
Per Year 	Sale 	Revenue 	(In Years) 	Value
3 	$200 	$600 	4 	$2,400
3 	$200 	$600 	5 	$3,000
3 	$200 	$600 	6 	$3,600
3 	$200 	$600 	7 	$4,200
3 	$200 	$600 	8 	$4 800	3 	$200 	$600 	8 	$4,800
3 	$200 	$600 	9 	$5,400

-- 7 of 38 --

Reality Check ...
The Good News:
If you’re a business owner, that means you make
more than SIX TIMES times more money 	in this	more than SIX TIMES times more money – in this
case, sales jump from $3 million to $18 million a
year	year.
The Great News:
If y 	’re a 	p w 	o marketing consultant	If you re a copywriter or marketing consultant
taking 10% of sales, you get $1.8 million a year.

-- 8 of 38 --

TIP:
To Make a Deal When a Business Owner	To Make a Deal When a Business Owner
Balks …
 Ask for 5% of sales and a 5% stock option.
 Ask for 5% of sales now and a 5% bonus at
year-end.
 Ask for a monthly retainer and a stock option
or performance bonus.

 Buy From 	More Often	 Buy From You More Often
 Spend More on Each Sale
 And Stay With 	Longer	 And Stay With You Longer …

personal contact	1. Superior products 	5. More
personal contact

frequent contact

types of contact

types of contact

bonding strategies

4. Top-notch customer service

-- 10 of 38 --

Customer File Basics

-- 11 of 38 --

Define “Active Customer:”
Typically , an active customer is a customer who
has made a purchase within the preceding twelve
months	months.
However, the definition varies from company to company, depending
on the type of business and typical customer buying cycles.

-- 12 of 38 --



-- 13 of 38 --

Promotions crafted to upgrade selected file segments
require that you be able to select groups of customers	require that you be able to select groups of customers
by certain key criteria …
 Age	 Age
 Sex
 Time on File	 Time on File
 Product Preferences
 Purchasing Frequency
 Average Purchase
 Largest Purchase
 Total Purchases
 Personal Data

-- 18 of 38 --

Activity Codes 101
The Activity Code (also called the “RFA Code” 	s	The Activity Code (also called the RFA Code ) is
a series of digits and/or letters that tells you
five crucial things about each active customer:	five crucial things about each active customer:
1. Recency: Time Since Last Order
2. Frequency: Average Number of Orders Placed Per Year
3. Amount 1 : Amount of AVERAGE Purchase Over Customer’s
Lifetime	Lifetime
4. Amount 2: Amount of LARGEST Purchase to Date
5 Amount 3 TOTAL Purchases to Date	5. Amount 3 : TOTAL Purchases to Date GCEFH
ency
ency
unt
1

2
unt
3

Freque
Amou
	Amou
	Amou

Activity Codes 101
Create a report that shows the number of	Create a report that shows the number of
customers in each Recency, Frequency and
Amount category RECENCY COUNTS
Recency 	Time Since
Code 	Last Purchase 	Quantity
Use this report to target file
segments for upgrading.

segments for upgrading.

-- 26 of 38 --

Armed with a functional customer file and detailed customer
records, you’re ready to begin systematically increasing the
value of your file.
First, a few rules ...

-- 27 of 38 --

Rule #1: Every sales effort to customers must
be designed to accomplish four crucial goals:	be designed to accomplish four crucial goals:
A. Strengthen the bond between the customer and
the company ...	the company ...
B. Produce a new piece of information about the
customer that can be used to target future
i	promotions ...
C. Upgrade the customer in terms of
recency frequency an 	r 	a 	ourse	recency, frequency and/or amount, and of course …
D. Produce maximum revenues.

-- 28 of 38 --

Rule #2: Every contact with a customer is an
opportunity to bond sell an 	gather customer	opportunity to bond, sell and gather customer
information:
A Outgoing promotions	A. Outgoing promotions
B. Receipt/Thank-You Letters
C Product Shipment Pa k 	i	C. Product Shipment Packaging
D. Customer Inquiries & Complaints
E. Opinion Polls and Surveys
F. Value-Added Gifts
G. Company Newsletters
H. Events (Teleseminars, Webinars, Live Events, etc.)

-- 29 of 38 --

Rule #3: Be willing to spend AT LEAST as much
to KEEP a customer as you’re willing to pay to	to KEEP a customer as you re willing to pay to
get a new customer.
Response rates are typically eight to ten times higher	Response rates are typically eight to ten times higher
when selling to customers than to prospects.
Avera e sale is t 	icall 	hi her as well.
Getting new customers involves significant capital risk;
Keeping a customer doesn’t.
Smart marketers are willing to pay
at least
four times more
to
keep a proven customer
than to get a new one.

-- 30 of 38 --

PERSPECTIVE:
If your average customer is with you for seven years	If your average customer is with you for seven years
and spends an average of $500 per year with you,
LOSING an average customer will cost you $3,500.

-- 31 of 38 --

Rule #4: First impressions mean a lot.
 Ensure that all new customer acquisition promotions begin the	 Ensure that all new customer acquisition promotions begin the
bonding process.
 Personal messaging
 Company positioning
 Create trust
 Use first product shipment to strengthen bonding.
 Customer-friendly service
 Fast delivery
 Personal “Thank-You” letter from the president
 Deliver an unadvertised bonus in first product ship ed	 Deliver an unadvertised bonus in first product shipped
 Craft a 30, 60 or 90-day “New Customer Experience” designed to
quickly generate a secondary sale while strengthening the new
relationship.

-- 32 of 38 --

Two Types of Customer File Promotions:
HORIZONTAL Promotions Tar et a Se ment of
Your File Based On Their Shared Characteristics
 RFA
 Birthdays & Anniversaries
 Age
 Sex	 Sex
 Location
 Time on File
 A Recent Order	 A Recent Order
VERTICAL Promotions Target the Entire File and
Are Triggered by Shared Events
 Holidays & Red-Letter Days
 Breaking News Events	 Breaking News Events
 New Product Announcements

contains all available data on
each customer

the most pressing challenges and
opportunities.

file management software is
optimal for storing and

at the beginning and end of their
active lifetimes.

selecting target customer
groups
